No documented platform-level security incidents are known.

Vouchley.history: Vouchley is a review platform for freelancers to collect and display client testimonials; no notable founding date or ownership events are documented.
Vouchley.summary_abuse: Vouchley is a freelancer review platform with no notable abuse signal. Review platforms can be gamed with fake reviews, but Vouchley has no specific documented abuse incidents.

Vox Media.history: Vox Media was founded in 2003 as SB Nation, a sports blogging network, and expanded into a digital media company operating The Verge, Vox, Polygon, Eater, and other publications. Its Chorus CMS platform is licensed to other publishers.
Vox Media.summary_abuse: Vox Media is a digital media company operating established news publications with no documented abuse cases involving its CMS platform.

Voximplant.history: Voximplant is a cloud communications PaaS providing voice, video, and messaging APIs for developers. It was founded around 2013 and serves developers building communication applications.
Voximplant.summary_abuse: Voximplant is a cloud communications platform. Voice and SMS APIs carry some risk of being used for robocalling or spam SMS, but Voximplant has no documented significant abuse history.

VP-ASP.history: VP-ASP is one of the earliest ASP-based shopping cart software packages, dating back to 1997. It was widely used in the early 2000s for online store creation but has since been largely abandoned as modern ecommerce platforms overtook it.
VP-ASP.summary_abuse: VP-ASP has no notable current abuse signal. It is a legacy abandoned ecommerce platform from the late 1990s with no active development or hosting service.

VReview.history: VReview is a Korean AI-powered review marketing SaaS for ecommerce. It uses customer video and photo reviews to boost conversion rates, primarily serving Korean online shopping malls.
VReview.summary_abuse: VReview has no notable abuse signal. It is a niche review marketing platform for Korean ecommerce, and its review collection features don't present clear vectors for scam or fraud use.

Vrio.history: Vrio is a headless ecommerce platform offering API-driven and hosted solutions for building and managing online stores; no notable ownership changes or major events on record.
Vrio.summary_abuse: Vrio is a niche headless ecommerce platform with no documented abuse signal. Its B2B SaaS focus and lack of a prominent free tier with custom-domain hosting reduce exposure to typical ecommerce fraud infrastructure abuse.

VTEX.history: VTEX was founded in 2000 in Brazil and grew to become one of the leading enterprise ecommerce platforms in Latin America. The company expanded globally and in 2021 went public on the NYSE under the ticker VTEX, raising over $361 million in its IPO. VTEX serves major global brands including Whirlpool, AB InBev, and Motorola.
VTEX.summary_abuse: VTEX is an enterprise ecommerce platform primarily used by large brands and retailers. Its enterprise-only model with dedicated onboarding makes it a low-risk abuse target compared to self-serve platforms. No significant documented abuse patterns are associated with VTEX; its customer base consists of established merchants with contractual agreements and identity verification.

Vtiger.history: Vtiger CRM was founded in 2004 in the US, initially as an open-source fork of SugarCRM. Over time it evolved into a commercial SaaS offering while retaining a self-hosted community edition. No major ownership changes are on record; the company remains independently operated.
Vtiger.summary_abuse: Vtiger is a CRM and business platform with no notable abuse signal. As a B2B SaaS tool, it does not expose consumer-facing surfaces commonly exploited by scammers, and its primary value is in managing customer relationships for legitimate businesses.

Vue Storefront.history: Vue Storefront was founded in 2017 in Poland as an open-source headless ecommerce frontend framework. The company raised VC funding and rebranded commercially as Alokai around 2023 while the open-source project continued.
Vue Storefront.summary_abuse: Vue Storefront is a headless ecommerce frontend framework with no notable abuse signal. It is developer infrastructure for building storefronts rather than a hosted consumer-facing service, limiting its abuse exposure.

Vue.ai.history: Vue.ai (formerly Mad Street Den) was founded in 2013 in the US by Ashwini Asokan and Anand Chandrasekaran to apply computer vision and AI to retail personalisation. The company rebranded to Vue.ai around 2019.
Vue.ai.summary_abuse: Vue.ai is an enterprise AI personalisation platform targeting retail brands. No documented abuse signal; it operates as a B2B service without consumer-facing surfaces commonly exploited in fraud or phishing schemes.

Vue.js.history: Vue.js was created by Evan You and first released in February 2014 after he extracted the best parts of Angular while working at Google. Vue 2 launched in 2016 and became one of the most-starred projects on GitHub. Vue 3 was released in 2020 with a Composition API and improved TypeScript support, becoming the recommended version in 2022.
Vue.js.summary_abuse: Vue.js is a foundational JavaScript UI framework with no abuse signal. It is open-source library infrastructure used to build web applications and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters target.

Vue2-animate.history: Vue2-animate is a Vue.js port of Animate.css created around 2016. It is a small utility library with no commercial backing and minimal maintenance activity.
Vue2-animate.summary_abuse: Vue2-animate is a tiny CSS animation helper for Vue.js with no abuse signal. It is pure frontend library code with no network or data-handling surface.

VuePress.history: VuePress was created by Evan You (creator of Vue.js) and first released in 2018 as a Vue-powered static site generator for technical documentation. VuePress 2 launched in 2022 with Vue 3 support.
VuePress.summary_abuse: VuePress is a static site generator for technical documentation with no abuse signal. Generated sites are self-hosted by developers and the tool itself has no hosted service surface exploitable by fraudsters.

VueStrap.history: VueStrap is a collection of Bootstrap components built with Vue.js, created around 2015. It saw limited adoption and has been largely superseded by more comprehensive Vue UI frameworks like Vuetify and BootstrapVue.
VueStrap.summary_abuse: VueStrap is a small Vue.js component library with no abuse signal. It is frontend library code with no hosted service surface.

Vuetify.history: Vuetify was created by John Leider and first released in 2016. It is a Material Design component framework for Vue.js that grew into one of the most popular Vue UI libraries. Vuetify 3 launched in 2022 with full Vue 3 and TypeScript support.
Vuetify.summary_abuse: Vuetify is a Vue.js UI component library with no abuse signal. It is open-source frontend library code with no hosted service surface that fraudsters would target.

Vuex.history: Vuex was created as the official state management library for Vue.js applications, first released alongside the Vue.js ecosystem around 2015. With the release of Vue 3, Pinia became the officially recommended state management solution, and Vuex 4 was released mainly for Vue 3 migration support. New projects are encouraged to use Pinia instead.
Vuex.summary_abuse: Vuex is a state management library for Vue.js with no abuse signal. It is JavaScript library infrastructure with no consumer-facing surface.

VuFind.history: VuFind was created at Villanova University Library in 2007 as an open-source library resource discovery portal. It is maintained by the broader library community and remains widely used in academic and public library institutions.
VuFind.summary_abuse: VuFind is a library resource discovery portal with no abuse signal. It is backend library infrastructure deployed in academic and public institutions with no consumer-facing surface exploitable by scammers.

Vultr.history: Vultr was founded in 2014 by David Aninowsky as a cloud infrastructure provider offering bare-metal and virtual private servers. It has grown into a mainstream cloud alternative to AWS, Azure, and GCP with global data center presence.
Vultr.summary_abuse: Vultr is a cloud hosting provider with some documented abuse exposure typical of IaaS platforms. Malicious actors occasionally use VPS instances for malware C2 servers, spam, and credential-stuffing attacks, as is common with any commodity cloud provider. Vultr publishes an AUP and has an abuse reporting contact, though enforcement responsiveness is considered moderate compared to hyperscalers.

Vuture.history: Vuture was founded in the UK around 2002 as a marketing technology platform specializing in professional services firms. It provides digital marketing communications tools tailored to law, accountancy, and consulting firms.
Vuture.summary_abuse: Vuture is a B2B marketing automation platform targeting professional services. No notable abuse signal; it operates as a closed enterprise tool without the anonymous sign-up or free-tier features commonly exploited for spam.

Vuukle.history: Vuukle was founded around 2015 as an audience engagement and commenting platform for publishers. It combines commenting, emojis, and social sharing tools with ad monetization features for media sites.
Vuukle.summary_abuse: Vuukle is a commenting and engagement platform for media publishers. Like most commenting systems, it can be exploited for spam comments and link injection, though this is primarily a content moderation challenge rather than large-scale fraud infrastructure. Vuukle's ad-network integration means it collects user behavioral data across sites.

Vvveb CMS.history: Vvveb CMS is an open-source PHP CMS with a visual drag-and-drop page builder, created around 2015 as a solo developer project. It has remained a niche project with an active GitHub presence but limited wider adoption.
Vvveb CMS.summary_abuse: Vvveb CMS is a self-hosted open-source content management system with low abuse signal. As a self-hosted PHP CMS, any abuse risk is governed by the site owner rather than the software vendor.

VWO.history: VWO (Visual Website Optimizer) was founded in 2009 by Sparsh Gupta and Paras Chopra in India as a product of Wingify. It is one of the first self-serve A/B testing platforms. VWO has remained independent and bootstrapped, growing to serve thousands of customers globally.
VWO.summary_abuse: VWO is a legitimate A/B testing and conversion optimization platform with no notable abuse patterns. Its embedded JavaScript tracking scripts are standard analytics-adjacent and do collect visitor behavioral data, which users should be aware of for GDPR consent purposes. No documented use in phishing or malicious campaigns.

VWO Engage.history: VWO Engage (formerly PushCrew) is the push notification product within the VWO platform by Wingify, an Indian software company. It was rebranded and integrated into the VWO product suite as a unified marketing automation tool for SaaS and ecommerce marketers.
VWO Engage.summary_abuse: VWO Engage is a push notification platform that sends browser push notifications to opted-in users. Push notification services have documented abuse potential including sending spam or phishing notifications through hijacked or deceptive subscription flows. VWO has acceptable use policies and focuses on legitimate marketing use cases. The risk is moderate given the nature of push notification delivery.

vxe-table.history: vxe-table is a Vue.js table component library created around 2018, maintained by the open-source developer xuliangzhan (Xu Liangzhan) in China. It supports complex data grid features including virtual scrolling, tree structures, editing, and printing. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events.
vxe-table.summary_abuse: vxe-table is a Vue.js data grid component with no notable abuse signal. It is a frontend library embedded in applications and has no consumer-facing surface that could be exploited for phishing or fraud.

Vyper.history: Vyper is a SaaS platform for running viral giveaways and contests to grow social media audiences, founded around 2013. It targets small businesses and marketers with growth-hacking tools.
Vyper.summary_abuse: Vyper is a giveaway and viral contest platform with low documented abuse signal. Such platforms can be misused to inflate social media follower counts or generate fake entries, but Vyper operates as a paid B2B service with account-level controls that limit large-scale abuse.

Vyve+.history: Vyve+ is an AI-powered marketing and sales automation platform. It appears to be a recently launched SaaS startup with limited public history.
Vyve+.summary_abuse: Vyve+ is a marketing automation platform with no documented abuse signal. As a paid B2B service, its primary risk is potential use for marketing spam, though it operates within standard commercial constraints.
